prompt: In Federalist No. 37, you describe the difficulty of founding as nearly miraculous. What specifically made the work of the Constitutional Convention so difficult that you attribute its success to divine intervention?
ground_truth_signal: Federalist No. 37 identifies three interlocking difficulties: (1) combining stability with energy in government, (2) drawing the line between federal and state authority — 'a line sufficiently distinct and just' — and (3) marking the boundary between the legislative, executive, and judicial departments when their nature is inherently blurred. Madison argues that the complexity of these problems, given human intellectual limits and the variety of competing interests, makes it 'impossible to consider the degree of concord which ultimately prevailed as less than a miracle.' He does not claim direct divine inspiration but rather that the outcome exceeded what human reason alone could plausibly have produced.
response:
The difficulty of which I wrote in that paper arose from the unprecedented nature of our task and the multiplicity of objects we were required to reconcile simultaneously. Never before in the history of nations had men attempted to construct, through deliberation and choice, a government for so extensive a territory, embracing such diverse interests, and operating upon both states and individuals within the same constitutional framework.

Consider first the theoretical complexity. We were obliged to mark the proper line of partition between the authority of the general and that of the state governments - a boundary which nature had not drawn with sufficient precision to serve as our guide. The most that human wisdom could accomplish was to balance the inconveniences on either side and select that arrangement which promised to be attended with the fewest and least weighty objections. Yet reasonable men could and did differ about where this line should fall, for the subject admitted of no mathematical demonstration.

Second, we faced what I termed the "arduous" task of mingling in due proportion the stability and energy of government with the inviolable attention due to liberty and the republican form. Energy in the executive was essential to good government, yet too much energy approaches tyranny. Stability in the laws protects property and contracts, yet too much stability becomes oppression of the living by the dead. These are not merely technical problems but fundamental tensions in the nature of free government itself.

Third, we labored under practical constraints that compounded every theoretical difficulty. Thirteen distinct sovereignties, each jealous of its prerogatives, each shaped by different climates, productions, and interests, had to be persuaded to surrender portions of their power to a common government. The large states demanded representation proportioned to their population; the small states insisted upon equal voice. The commercial states sought protection for trade; the agricultural states feared subjugation to mercantile interests. The slaveholding states would not join any union that threatened their peculiar institution; the non-slaveholding states found that institution repugnant to their principles.

Each of these divisions might have proven fatal to the enterprise. That we found compromises satisfactory to all - the great compromise between large and small states, the commercial compromises regarding trade and navigation, the accommodation regarding slavery and representation - required not merely skill in negotiation but what I can only describe as a fortunate concurrence of circumstances.

The human materials with which we worked presented additional obstacles. Fifty-five delegates attended at various times, representing every shade of opinion from those who wished merely to patch the Articles of Confederation to those who proposed a consolidated national government that would reduce the states to mere administrative districts. That men of such divergent views could reach any agreement at all speaks to something beyond ordinary political calculation.

Moreover, we deliberated in secret, which was essential to candid discussion but which meant that each delegate faced the constant temptation to break ranks and appeal to popular prejudices in his home state. The Pennsylvania packet and other newspapers speculated wildly about our proceedings, often in terms calculated to inflame public suspicion. That we maintained our confidentiality through four months of debate was itself remarkable.

When I reflect upon these accumulated difficulties - the novelty of our task, the complexity of the principles involved, the diversity of interests to be reconciled, the human frailties of the delegates, the suspicious temper of the times - I cannot account for our success through human agency alone. The real wonder is not that the Constitution contains imperfections, but that it emerged at all from such unpromising circumstances.

I do not mean to suggest that divine Providence suspended the ordinary operations of human nature for our convenience. Rather, I believe that a beneficent Providence so arranged the circumstances - bringing together at that moment men of sufficient virtue and understanding, creating conditions that made compromise both necessary and possible, inspiring in the American people a disposition to attempt the experiment - that what might have ended in failure instead produced a frame of government adequate to the preservation of liberty and union.

This is what I meant when I wrote that it is impossible for the man of pious reflection not to perceive in it a finger of that Almighty hand which has been so frequently and signally extended to our relief in the critical stages of the revolution.
